name: Bhartrhari
dates_notes: Dated by the Chinese pilgrim I-tsing's reference; the Vākyapadīya survives.
core_thesis: Bhartṛhari, primarily a grammarian-philosopher in the Pāṇinian tradition, advances *śabda-advaita* (word-non-dualism): the ultimate is *śabda-brahman*, an undivided word-principle from which the manifest world of meanings, objects, and cognitions emerges through the apparent transformation (*vivarta*) of differentiation (*bheda*). Vākyapadīya 1.1 — *anādi-nidhanaṃ brahma śabda-tattvaṃ yad akṣaram / vivartate 'rtha-bhāvena prakriyā jagato yataḥ* — frames *brahman* as beginningless, endless, imperishable word-reality which appears (*vivartate*) as the world-process. Cognition is intrinsically linguistic: there is no awareness (*pratyaya*) unsuffused by *śabda*. The integral *vākya* (sentence) is meaning-bearing and yields a single instantaneous *pratibhā* (intuitive flash); the analyzed *pada* and *varṇa* are abstractions. Three levels of speech — *paśyantī* (seeing/intuitive), *madhyamā* (intermediate/mental), *vaikharī* (articulated) — order the descent from undifferentiated word-principle to spoken sound. The position is cross-tradition: it is grammatical (*Vyākaraṇa-darśana*), but its *brahman*-ontology and *vivarta* idiom feed directly into Maṇḍana and Advaita.

engaged_works.title: Vakyapadiya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; engaged here only insofar as it is Vedāntic in implication, especially the śabda-brahman doctrine that influences Maṇḍana.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus on Bhartṛhari's authorship of the Vākyapadīya.

engaged_works.summary: A *prakaraṇa* in three *kāṇḍas* (hence *Trikāṇḍī*): *Brahma-Kāṇḍa* / *Āgama-Samuccaya* (1) — the metaphysics of *śabda-brahman*, the *sphoṭa* doctrine of meaning-bearing linguistic units, the levels of *vāk*; *Vākya-Kāṇḍa* (2) — the integral sentence as primary, theories of meaning, the doctrine of *pratibhā*; *Pada-Kāṇḍa* / *Prakīrṇaka* (3) — analytic treatment of nominal categories (*jāti*, *dravya*, *saṃbandha*, *sādhana*, *kriyā*, *kāla*, *puruṣa*, *saṃkhyā*, etc.). Bhartṛhari's own *vṛtti* survives for kāṇḍas 1 and 2 (authorship of the *vṛtti* on Kāṇḍa 1 is debated). The work matters for Vedānta because the *vivarta* terminology — *brahman* appearing as a manifold without itself undergoing real transformation — and the conception of an undifferentiated linguistic absolute that grounds all cognitive content directly shape Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* and indirectly the Advaita technical lexicon. Engaged here only insofar as it is Vedāntic in implication; the bulk of the work is grammatical-philosophical and outside the strict Vedāntic *prasthāna*.
